intro.kicker: Topic 17 of 22 • Chapter: Newton’s Laws of Motion • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: Analyses two or three blocks pushed together (direct contact, no string) on a smooth horizontal surface. By treating the entire system together and then isolating each body with a FBD, the common acceleration a = F/(m₁+m₂) and the contact force (normal force between blocks) N = m₂F/(m₁+m₂) are derived. The key NEET insight: the contact force transmitted between blocks is always less than the applied force F.

expectedQuestions.description: Regularly tested in NEET as numerical MCQs asking for contact force or acceleration of a multi-block system on a frictionless surface.

timeRequired.description: Two-block derivation — 20 min; three-block extension — 20 min; directional case (force applied from other end) — 15 min.

difficulty.description: Acceleration calculation is straightforward once the system is treated as a whole. The contact force calculation requires an individual FBD of one block — this is the step most students skip and then get wrong.

nriUsaGap.description: US AP Physics covers contact forces qualitatively but NEET tests the exact formula for contact force between specific pairs with three blocks, which demands careful block-by-block FBD analysis.

bulletNotes: The switch from F applied on left vs right changes which block pair carries the larger contact force.
Three-block contact force (f₁ vs f₂) is a frequent NEET differentiation question.

strategySteps.title: System-First Approach for Acceleration
strategySteps.description: Always compute acceleration first by treating all blocks as one system: a = F / (m₁ + m₂ + ... + mn). This uses Newton’s 2nd Law for the whole without needing individual contact forces.

strategySteps.title: Isolate One Block for Contact Force
strategySteps.description: To find f between blocks A and B, draw a FBD of the single block farthest from the applied force. The only horizontal force on that block is the contact force pushing it forward. Set f = (mass of isolated block) × a.

strategySteps.title: Direction of Applied Force Matters
strategySteps.description: If force F is applied to the left block (A), contact force between A and B: f = m₂ a = m₂ F/(m₁+m₂). If F is applied to right block (B), reverse the formula: f = m₁ a = m₁ F/(m₁+m₂).

cards.title: Blocks in Contact — Complete Notes
cards.description: Two-block and three-block cases with FBDs, contact force derivations for all force-direction configurations, and NEET-level solved numericals.

subtopics.name: Two Blocks — Force on Left Block
Two Blocks — Force on Right Block
Three Blocks — f₁ and f₂ Analysis

cards.title: Two Blocks — Force Applied to Block A
cards.coreRevision: Force F applied to m₁, with m₂ in contact. System acceleration: a = F/(m₁+m₂). Contact force on m₂: f = m₂ × a = m₂ F/(m₁+m₂).
points: f is always less than F since m₂ < (m₁+m₂)

cards.title: Two Blocks — Force Applied to Block B
cards.coreRevision: If F is applied on the right block (m₂) instead, the acceleration is the same: a = F/(m₁+m₂). Contact force on m₁ (isolated) = m₁ a = m₁ F/(m₁+m₂).
points: Swapping the direction of F swaps the numerator mass in the contact-force formula
cards.example: m₁ = 3 kg, m₂ = 2 kg, F = 10 N applied to m₂: f = 3×2 = 6 N (larger than case 1).

cards.title: Three Blocks — f₁ and f₂
cards.coreRevision: F applied to m₁, blocks m₂ and m₃ in series behind it. Common: a = F/(m₁+m₂+m₃). Contact force between m₁ and m₂: f₁ = (m₂+m₃)F/(m₁+m₂+m₃). Contact force between m₂ and m₃: f₂ = m₃ F/(m₁+m₂+m₃).
points: f₁ > f₂ because more mass is being pushed by the f₁ force

curriculum.hintText: Contact force between touching blocks requires systematic FBD isolation, which US AP Physics teaches but rarely tests for three-body cases.

gaps.title: Three-Body Contact Force Differentiation
gaps.description: US AP Physics C typically stops at two-body systems in contact force problems. NEET extends to three blocks and asks for a specific contact force between a specified pair.
bullets: Always draw a separate FBD for the block (or sub-group of blocks) on the side farthest from F.
The contact force = (sum of masses on that side) × a.

gaps.title: Force Direction Sensitivity
gaps.description: NEET explicitly tests both directions of F application on the same block stack. US curricula rarely vary this parameter in a practice problem.
bullets: Practice both F-from-left and F-from-right versions of the same two-block problem.
Notice that the contact force formula numerator contains only the mass(es) being pushed, not the mass being pulled.

questions.question: Three blocks of equal mass m = 2 kg are placed in contact on a frictionless surface. A force F = 12 N is applied on the first block. The force with which the second block pushes the third is:
questions.explanation: a = 12/(2+2+2) = 2 m/s². Contact force between 2nd and 3rd block = m₃ × a = 2 × 2 = 4 N.

questions.question: Two blocks of masses 4 kg and 6 kg are pressed against each other by a force F = 10 N from the 4 kg side. The contact force between the blocks is:
questions.explanation: a = 10/10 = 1 m/s². Contact force on 6 kg block: f = 6 × 1 = 6 N.

questions.question: Four blocks of mass 1, 2, 3, 4 kg are in contact and pushed by a force of 20 N on the 1 kg end. Find the contact force between the 3 kg and 4 kg blocks.
In the two-block (m₁ = 3 kg, m₂ = 7 kg) problem, if F = 50 N is applied to m₂, what is the contact force acting on m₁?

items.title: Global system acceleration first
items.description: Treat all n blocks as one body: a = F/(total mass). Do this before attempting any contact force.

items.title: Isolate the farthest sub-system for contact force
items.description: For f between blocks A and B, draw FBD of only the block(s) on the far side of the boundary. F_net on them = contact force.

items.title: Recognise directional dependence
items.description: Changing which block F is applied on changes the numerator in the contact force formula. Derive both explicitly.

items.title: Verify: contact force < applied force
items.description: In a correctly solved problem, every contact force is less than F. Use this as a quick sanity check.

items.question: Why is the contact force between blocks less than the applied force F?
items.answer: The contact force only needs to accelerate the remaining sub-system (blocks beyond the contact point). Since that sub-system has smaller mass than the total, f = (smaller mass) × a < F = (total mass) × a.

items.question: Why does the contact force formula change when F switches direction?
items.answer: The contact force acts on the sub-system on one particular side of the contact. Which sub-system that is depends on the direction of F — it is always the sub-system that the contact force is pushing. Different direction, different sub-system, different formula.

items.question: Can the surface have friction in this topic?
items.answer: In this OCR-grounded topic, the surface is smooth (frictionless). All derivations assume zero friction. With friction, each block’s FBD must include a frictional force and the formulas change significantly.

grounding_summary: Page 197 presents a table (Table 4.2) for Motion of Blocks in Contact with two-block and three-block configurations including equations and contact force formulas.
Page 198 continues with the ABC three-block configuration and the reversed-force case, confirming formulas for all four sub-configurations.
Research confirms this is a constant NEET topic with 1 question per year targeting contact force calculation.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part I — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion
sources.why_used: Foundation source for Newton’s Second Law application to systems of bodies in contact; confirms FBD methodology for contact forces.

sources.title: Laws of Motion — NEET Detailed Explanation (EduRev)
sources.why_used: Confirms that contact force between blocks in a pushed series is tested regularly in NEET and provides concept-level clarity on FBD isolation per block.

review.notes: Content grounded in pages 197–198 which contain the complete blocks-in-contact table. All four configurations (2-block left, 2-block right, 3-block, 3-block reversed) covered. Formulas verified from OCR table data.
